content: If the unit vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are inclined at $2 \theta$ and $|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}|<1$, then if $0<\theta<\pi, \theta$ lies in the interval.
explanation:
$\because a$ and $b$ are unit vectors.

We have,

$$\begin{aligned} |a-b| & =\sqrt{(a-b)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(a-b) \cdot(a-b)} \\ & =\sqrt{|a|^2+|b|^2-2 a \cdot b} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{1+1-2 \cos 2 \theta} \\ & =\sqrt{2(1-\cos 2 \theta)}=2|\sin \theta| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \because|a-b|<1 \Rightarrow|\sin \theta|<\frac{1}{2} \\ & \text { Hence, } \theta \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{6}\right) \text { or }\left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}, \pi\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: The volume of the parallelopiped whose edges are represented by $\mathbf{a}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, $\mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\mathbf{c}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ is
explanation:
The volume of a parallelepiped can be calculated using the scalar triple product of its defining vectors: $[ \mathbf{a} \, \mathbf{b} \, \mathbf{c} ] = \mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})$.

First, compute the cross product $\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}$:

$ \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} $

Calculate the determinant:

$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c} = & \hat{\mathbf{i}} (2 \times 2 - (-1) \times (-1)) - \hat{\mathbf{j}} (1 \times 2 - (-1) \times 3) + \hat{\mathbf{k}} (1 \times (-1) - 3 \times 2) \\ = & \hat{\mathbf{i}}(4 - 1) - \hat{\mathbf{j}}(2 + 3) + \hat{\mathbf{k}}(-1 - 6) \\ = & 3 \hat{\mathbf{i}} - 5 \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 7 \hat{\mathbf{k}} \end{aligned} $

Next, find $\mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})$:

$ \mathbf{a} = 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} - 3 \hat{\mathbf{j}} + 4 \hat{\mathbf{k}} $

$ \mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) = (2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} - 3 \hat{\mathbf{j}} + 4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot (3 \hat{\mathbf{i}} - 5 \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) $

Calculate the dot product:

$ \begin{aligned} = & (3 \times 2) + (-3 \times -5) + (4 \times -7) \\ = & 6 + 15 - 28 \\ = & -7 \end{aligned} $

Therefore, the volume of the parallelepiped is $|-7|$, which is 7.

content: A unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors $$\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ is
Let $$a, b$$ and $$c$$ be three unit vectors such that $$a \times(b \times c)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(b+c)$$. If $$b$$ is not parallel to $$c$$, then the angle between $$a$$ and $$b$$ is
explanation:
$$\because|a|=|b|=|c|=1$$

and $$a \times(b \times c)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(b+c)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad a \times(b \times c)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(b+c) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(a \cdot c) b-(a \cdot b) c=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} b+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} c \end{aligned}$$

On comparing, we get

$$\begin{gathered} a \cdot b=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow|a||b| \cos \theta=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{5 \pi}{6} \end{gathered}$$

content: A unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}$$ and $$\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ is
